source_paper_title: A Nonparametric Framework for Online Stochastic Matching with Correlated Arrivals
context:
Section 7 (Summary and Future Directions), page 30.

Finally, a very concrete problem from our paper is whether our 1/2-competitive guarantee for Indep\(\cap\) Adv can be improved under Indep \(\cap\) Rand, where the arrival order is random.
title: Determine best competitive ratio for online stochastic matching with independent demand random order
background_latex:
Consider an online stochastic matching problem with resources indexed by \(i\in[n]\), each with initial inventory \(k_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\), and query types indexed by \(j\in[m]\). Matching one unit of resource \(i\) to a query of type \(j\) yields known reward \(r_{i,j}\ge 0\) (with \(r_{i,j}=0\) allowing incompatibility). 

Demand uncertainty is modeled by a random demand vector \(D=(D_1,\dots,D_m)\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}^m\), where \(D_j\) is the total number of queries of type \(j\) that will arrive over the horizon. In the \(\mathrm{Indep}\) model, the coordinates \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) are mutually independent and each \(D_j\) has an arbitrary (nonparametric) distribution known to the decision-maker.

Conditional on the realized \(D\), the arrival sequence \(\sigma\) is generated by taking a uniformly random permutation of the multiset with \(D_j\) copies of each type \(j\) (random-order model, denoted \(\mathrm{Rand}\)). The sequence length is \(|\sigma|=\sum_{j=1}^m D_j\).

An online algorithm observes arrivals sequentially in the order given by \(\sigma\) and must irrevocably either match the current query to an available resource unit (reducing the corresponding inventory by 1) or reject it. The algorithm knows \((r_{i,j})\), \((k_i)\), and the distributional model (including that arrivals are in random order), but not the realized \(D\) or \(\sigma\) in advance.

For any realized demand vector \(D\), let \(\mathrm{OFF}(D)\) denote the offline optimal total reward achievable with full knowledge of all arrivals (i.e., the maximum-weight matching/assignment subject to inventories). Define \(\mathrm{OFF}(I)=\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\), where the expectation is over the randomness of \(D\). For an online algorithm \(\mathrm{ALG}\), define its expected reward on instance \(I\in\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) by \(\mathrm{ALG}(I)=\mathbb{E}_{D,\sigma}[\mathrm{ALG}(D,\sigma)]\), including any algorithmic randomness.

For a family of instances \(\mathcal{I}\), the (worst-case) competitive ratio of an algorithm is \(\inf_{I\in\mathcal{I}} \mathrm{ALG}(I)/\mathrm{OFF}(I)\), and the best achievable competitive ratio is the supremum of this quantity over all online algorithms.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether, under the \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) model, the optimal (worst-case) competitive ratio against the offline benchmark \(\mathrm{OFF}(I)\) is strictly larger than \(1/2\); equivalently, determine the best achievable value of
\[
\sup_{\text{online algorithms }\mathrm{ALG}}\ \inf_{I\in\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}} \frac{\mathrm{ALG}(I)}{\mathrm{OFF}(I)}
\]
and in particular whether it exceeds \(1/2\).
latex:
Consider an online stochastic matching problem with resources indexed by \(i\in[n]\), each with initial inventory \(k_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\), and query types indexed by \(j\in[m]\). Matching one unit of resource \(i\) to a query of type \(j\) yields known reward \(r_{i,j}\ge 0\) (with \(r_{i,j}=0\) allowing incompatibility). 

Demand uncertainty is modeled by a random demand vector \(D=(D_1,\dots,D_m)\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}^m\), where \(D_j\) is the total number of queries of type \(j\) that will arrive over the horizon. In the \(\mathrm{Indep}\) model, the coordinates \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) are mutually independent and each \(D_j\) has an arbitrary (nonparametric) distribution known to the decision-maker.

Conditional on the realized \(D\), the arrival sequence \(\sigma\) is generated by taking a uniformly random permutation of the multiset with \(D_j\) copies of each type \(j\) (random-order model, denoted \(\mathrm{Rand}\)). The sequence length is \(|\sigma|=\sum_{j=1}^m D_j\).

An online algorithm observes arrivals sequentially in the order given by \(\sigma\) and must irrevocably either match the current query to an available resource unit (reducing the corresponding inventory by 1) or reject it. The algorithm knows \((r_{i,j})\), \((k_i)\), and the distributional model (including that arrivals are in random order), but not the realized \(D\) or \(\sigma\) in advance.

For any realized demand vector \(D\), let \(\mathrm{OFF}(D)\) denote the offline optimal total reward achievable with full knowledge of all arrivals (i.e., the maximum-weight matching/assignment subject to inventories). Define \(\mathrm{OFF}(I)=\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\), where the expectation is over the randomness of \(D\). For an online algorithm \(\mathrm{ALG}\), define its expected reward on instance \(I\in\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) by \(\mathrm{ALG}(I)=\mathbb{E}_{D,\sigma}[\mathrm{ALG}(D,\sigma)]\), including any algorithmic randomness.

For a family of instances \(\mathcal{I}\), the (worst-case) competitive ratio of an algorithm is \(\inf_{I\in\mathcal{I}} \mathrm{ALG}(I)/\mathrm{OFF}(I)\), and the best achievable competitive ratio is the supremum of this quantity over all online algorithms.

Determine whether, under the \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) model, the optimal (worst-case) competitive ratio against the offline benchmark \(\mathrm{OFF}(I)\) is strictly larger than \(1/2\); equivalently, determine the best achievable value of
\[
\sup_{\text{online algorithms }\mathrm{ALG}}\ \inf_{I\in\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}} \frac{\mathrm{ALG}(I)}{\mathrm{OFF}(I)}
\]
and in particular whether it exceeds \(1/2\).
open_problem_quote: Finally, a very concrete problem from our paper is whether our 1/2-competitive guarantee for Indep\(\cap\) Adv can be improved under Indep \(\cap\) Rand, where the arrival order is random.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: To refute improvability beyond 1/2, one can search for finite instances (small n,m, discrete demand distributions) that minimize the ratio of candidate algorithms or that suggest upper-bound constructions; evaluating OFF and simulating an algorithm is computationally straightforward on small sizes. Automated search may plausibly uncover hard instances indicating an upper bound of 1/2 or close to it.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize families of online policies (e.g., LP-based routing rules, thresholds, adaptivity features) and use simulation on sampled Indep∩Rand instances to optimize the worst-case observed ratio as a fitness signal. However, the true objective is a worst-case guarantee over all instances, so simulation-based fitness is only a proxy and may overfit to sampled instances without yielding a rigorous bound.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper of Aouad and Ma introduces a nonparametric demand-vector view of online stochastic matching, separating uncertainty in the demand counts \(D=(D_1,\dots,D_m)\) from the arrival order (adversarial or random permutation). In their \(\mathrm{Indep}\) model (independent coordinates \(D_j\) with arbitrary distributions), they prove that the usual fluid LP based only on \(\mathbb E[D_j]\) can be arbitrarily loose, and they replace it with a tighter truncated LP using constraints of the form \(\sum_{i\in S} x_{i,j} \le \mathbb E[\min\{D_j,\sum_{i\in S} k_i\}]\). With a new lossless rounding scheme that avoids the positive correlations created by naive independent routing when \(D_j\) is random, they obtain a tight \(1/2\)-competitive algorithm for \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Adv}\), and explicitly pose as an open question whether the guarantee can be improved under \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) (random permutation of the realized multiset).

Among the forward citations provided, none resolves this open question in the exact model/benchmark \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\). The most relevant progress comes from the probe-commit/prophet-matching line: "Prophet matching in the probe-commit model" and "Online bipartite matching in the probe-commit model" show that random order plus independent arrival distributions can enable \(1-1/e\) guarantees, but crucially against an offline adaptive probing benchmark and with additional stochastic structure (edge existence/probing constraints) rather than fixed rewards \(r_{i,j}\) and random demand counts. These works nevertheless suggest that configuration LPs and random-order contention resolution can sometimes beat \(1/2\), and they sharpen understanding of when random order helps (and when it does not, via tight \(1/2\) and \(1/e\) bounds in their own settings).

The remaining citations are best viewed as tools and adjacent evidence. One-sided differential-equation methods ("Extending wormald's differential equation method to one-sided bounds") may be useful for proving upper bounds on any online algorithm by tracking suitable process variables under random order. Several papers develop attenuation and ordered contention resolution ideas in stochastic matching/probing models (e.g., "Improved guarantees for offline stochastic matching via new ordered contention resolution schemes" and related applied/market-design variants), but they do not translate directly to the \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) demand-vector model. Overall, based on the provided forward-citation set, the best-known worst-case guarantee for \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) against \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\) remains \(1/2\) (inherited from the \(\mathrm{Adv}\) result), and whether the optimal ratio is strictly larger than \(1/2\) under random order remains open.

sources.title: Prophet matching in the probe-commit model
sources.theorem_or_result: Borodin–MacRury–Rakheja (2022) study online bipartite stochastic matching in the probe-commit model with known independent (not necessarily identical) online arrivals and random order. Their main guarantee (Theorem 3) is that Algorithm 9 achieves expected weight at least \((1-1/e)\,\mathrm{OPT}(H_{\mathrm{typ}},(D_i)_{i=1}^n)\), where \(\mathrm{OPT}\) is the offline adaptive probing benchmark. They also show a tight \(1/2\) ratio for adversarial order (Theorem 1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the best worst-case competitive ratio in online stochastic matching under independent demand with random-order arrivals, measured against the offline omniscient optimum \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\), and in particular whether it is strictly larger than \(1/2\). This paper proves a \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio (>\(1/2\)) but in a different model/benchmark: (i) it is a prophet/known-i.d. vertex-arrival model rather than the demand-vector random-permutation model; (ii) it includes stochastic edge existence and probe-commit constraints; and crucially (iii) it benchmarks against an offline *adaptive probing* optimum, not the offline full-information assignment \(\mathrm{OFF}(D)\). Thus it does not resolve the stated open problem, but it provides techniques (configuration LP + contention resolution) and evidence that random order can yield ratios >\(1/2\) under independence assumptions, albeit for a different benchmark.

sources.title: Online bipartite matching in the probe-commit model
sources.theorem_or_result: In the probe-commit online stochastic matching model with known independently distributed (known i.d.) online vertices and random-order arrivals, the paper gives an efficient online algorithm with competitive ratio \(1-1/e\) against the offline adaptive probing benchmark \(\mathrm{OPT}\) (Corollary 5.6). It also gives a \(1/2\)-competitive algorithm for known i.d. with adversarial order (Corollary 5.5), and shows an optimal \(1/e\) ratio for edge-weighted worst-case instances with random-order arrivals (Theorem 6.3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether, in an online stochastic matching model with independent demand counts \(D_j\) and random-order arrivals of query types (Indep\(\cap\)Rand), the optimal competitive ratio versus \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\) is strictly larger than \(1/2\). This paper studies a different but related stochastic matching framework: online bipartite matching in the probe-commit model with edge existence probabilities and (known i.d.) independent draws of online vertex types, plus random-order arrivals. It proves a \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio in that setting, which is >\(1/2\), but the benchmark is the offline adaptive probing optimum \(\mathrm{OPT}\), not \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\), and the uncertainty is on edge activations rather than on the demand vector \(D\) with fixed rewards \(r_{i,j}\). Thus it does not resolve the stated open problem, though it provides techniques (configuration LP + contention resolution in random order) that may be relevant for improving beyond \(1/2\) in related random-order stochastic matching models.

sources.title: Extending wormald's differential equation method to one-sided bounds
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a one-sided (upper-bound) analogue of Wormald’s differential equation method for discrete-time stochastic processes. Main result (Theorem 3): for a cooperative Lipschitz system 
\[y'_j(t)=f_j(t,y_1(t),\dots,y_a(t)),\quad y_j(0)=\tilde y_j,\]
if adapted random variables \(Z_j(i)\) satisfy bounded increments and a one-sided trend condition 
\(\mathbb E[\Delta Z_j(i)\mid \mathcal H_i]\le f_j(i/n, Z_1(i)/n,\dots,Z_a(i)/n)+\delta\),
with initial condition \(Z_j(0)\le n y_j(0)+\lambda\), then with probability at least \(1-2a\exp\big(-\lambda^2/(2(b\sigma n+2\beta\lambda))\big)\) one has for all \(0\le i\le \min\{I,\sigma n\}\):
\[Z_j(i)\le n y_j(i/n)+3\lambda e^{2Li/n}.\]
It also recovers a two-sided version (Theorem 10) comparable to standard Wormald/Warnke bounds.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the optimal competitive ratio in online stochastic matching under \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\), in particular whether it can exceed \(1/2\). This paper does not analyze that matching model or prove any competitive-ratio upper/lower bound. However, it develops a technical tool explicitly motivated by (and previously used in) impossibility/hardness proofs for random-order online matching/contension-resolution settings (Section 1.1), including results cited as giving \(1/2\)-type impossibility bounds in related models. Such one-sided differential-equation bounds can help upper bound the performance of any online algorithm by tracking a process variable \(Z(i)\) with only an inequality on its drift, which is a common step in proving hardness results. Thus it is relevant as methodology that could potentially be used to prove upper bounds (e.g., \(\le 1/2\)) in the \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) matching setting, but it does not itself resolve whether the best ratio is \(>1/2\).

sources.title: Trading the system efficiency for the income equality of drivers in rideshare
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies an online bipartite matching-with-probing model under i.i.d. (KIID) arrivals with edge existence probabilities and patience constraints, and gives LP-based online algorithms with constant competitive ratios against an LP benchmark. In particular, for parameters \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\) with \(\alpha+\beta\le 1\):
- WarmUp achieves profit and fairness ratios at least \(\big(\alpha\tfrac{1-1/e}{2},\,\beta\tfrac{1-1/e}{2}\big)\).
- AttenAlg improves this to \(\big(\alpha\tfrac{e-1}{e+1},\,\beta\tfrac{e-1}{e+1}\big)\approx (0.46\alpha,0.46\beta)\).
It also proves hardness (w.r.t. these LP benchmarks) that no algorithm can have \(\alpha>0.51\) or \(\beta>0.51\) (and \(\alpha+\beta>1\) is impossible) simultaneously for the bi-objective setting.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for the optimal worst-case competitive ratio in online stochastic matching under \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) (independent demand counts with random-order permutation), and specifically whether it can exceed \(1/2\) against \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\).

This citing paper does not analyze the \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) model nor the same offline benchmark \(\mathrm{OFF}(I)=\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\). Instead it assumes i.i.d. arrivals over a fixed horizon \(T\) (KIID) and uses different benchmarks (LP relaxations for profit/fairness) in a stochastic matching-with-probing setting with cancellations (edge existence probabilities) and patience constraints.

Nevertheless, it is tangentially relevant: it provides LP-based online techniques (dependent rounding + attenuation) and constant-factor guarantees in a stochastic matching model with independent randomness, and it discusses upper bounds around \(\approx 0.51\) but only for its own LP benchmarks and bi-objective formulation. It therefore does not resolve whether the optimal competitive ratio under \(\mathrm{Indep}\cap\mathrm{Rand}\) exceeds \(1/2\), but offers related algorithmic ideas and (non-transferable) hardness intuition.

sources.title: Improved guarantees for offline stochastic matching via new ordered contention resolution schemes
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies offline stochastic matching with independently active edges and probing/timeout (patience) constraints, and gives improved approximation guarantees via ordered/random-order contention resolution schemes. Main guarantees include: (i) for general graphs with minimum patience at least 2, a random-order probing algorithm (Algorithm 1 with an attenuation function) that achieves approximation ratio \(\beta^*\approx 0.38278\) against the standard LP upper bound (Theorem 1); (ii) with infinite patience, a random-order probing algorithm achieving \((1-e^{-2})/2\approx 0.432\) (Corollary 5); (iii) for bipartite graphs with a unit-patience side, an algorithm achieving \(1-1/e\approx 0.632\) (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the best possible competitive ratio for an online stochastic matching model with independent demand counts \(D_j\) and random-order arrivals of query types, benchmarked against \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\), and asks whether the optimal ratio exceeds \(1/2\). This citing paper does not analyze that demand-arrival model or the \(\mathrm{OFF}(I)=\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\) benchmark. Instead it studies a different stochastic matching setting: edges exist independently with known probabilities and the algorithm probes edges (possibly in random order) under patience constraints, with performance measured as an approximation to the optimal probing policy (or to an LP relaxation). While both lines use random-order processing and attenuation/contention-resolution ideas, the results here are approximation ratios \(<1/2\) for general graphs (0.382/0.432) and do not address whether \(>1/2\) is achievable in the Indep∩Rand demand model. At best, the paper provides techniques (attenuation, random-order contention resolution) that could be relevant tools for designing/analyzing online algorithms under random order, but it neither proves a \(>1/2\) guarantee nor a \(\le 1/2\) impossibility for the open problem’s model.

sources.title: Exploring the tradeoff between competitive ratio and variance in online-matching markets
sources.theorem_or_result: In the KIID arrival model for multi-budgeted online stochastic matching (MB-OSM) with edge sparsity \(\Delta\), the paper gives LP-based online policies SAMP(\(\alpha\)) and ATT(\(\alpha\)) achieving expected-utility competitive ratio \((1-e^{-\alpha\Delta})/\Delta\) with respect to a benchmark LP upper bound on a clairvoyant optimum (Theorems 1–2,5). It also proves an LP-relative hardness upper bound \(\bigl(1-e^{-(\Delta-1+1/\Delta)}\bigr)/(\Delta-1+1/\Delta)\) for certain \(\Delta\) (Theorem 3), and shows under large budgets \(B\) the ratio approaches 1: for \(\Delta=1\), \(1-\sqrt{1/(2\pi B)}(1+o(1))\), and for general \(\Delta\), \(1-\kappa\sqrt{\ln\Delta/B}(1+o(1))\) (Theorem 4).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the best worst-case competitive ratio for online stochastic matching when the demand vector has independent coordinates and arrivals are in random order (Indep\(\cap\)Rand), and asks whether it can exceed \(1/2\) against \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\). This paper studies a different stochastic-arrival model (KIID: i.i.d. sampling with replacement over \(T\) rounds) and a different benchmark (an LP upper bound on a clairvoyant optimum under KIID), not the Indep\(\cap\)Rand model nor the \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\) benchmark. Its results therefore do not resolve whether the optimal ratio in Indep\(\cap\)Rand exceeds \(1/2\). However, it develops LP-based sampling/attenuation techniques and competitive-ratio bounds (e.g., \(1-1/e\) when \(\Delta=1\)) that are conceptually related to stochastic matching and may inform algorithm design/analysis in random-order settings.

sources.title: Online matching frameworks under stochastic rewards, product ranking, and unknown patience
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves competitive-ratio guarantees for online bipartite matching with stochastic edge existence (“stochastic rewards”) and patience constraints under several arrival models. In particular, for known IID arrivals it gives an online algorithm with competitive ratio

sources.connection: The open problem concerns the best possible competitive ratio for online stochastic matching when the demand vector has independent coordinates and the realized multiset of types is presented in uniformly random order (Indep\(\cap\)Rand), with performance measured against \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{OFF}(D)]\) (offline optimum with full knowledge of the realized multiset). This paper studies different stochastic matching models: stochastic edge existence (probe-commit) with known arrival distributions (IID or non-stationary “prophet arrivals”) and also adversarial arrivals, and it benchmarks against an offline policy that knows the realized sequence of types but not edge realizations. It does not analyze the Indep\(\cap\)Rand demand model nor the specific question of whether the optimal ratio exceeds \(1/2\) in that model. Thus it does not resolve the open problem, though its LP/policy framework and \((1-1/e)\)-type guarantees for IID arrivals are conceptually related to random-order/IID arrival analyses.
